dashboard (unauthenticated for now). This confirms the tunnel works.

If the hostname doesn't resolve, DNS may not have propagated yet — wait and
retry. Check with `dig suri.yokedostrich.com` (it should be a CNAME to
`<UUID>.cfargotunnel.com`).

Once it works, stop the foreground `cloudflared` with **Ctrl-C** — we'll
install it as a service next.

---

## Step 8 — Add Cloudflare Access (authentication)

Without this, the dashboard is open to the internet with no login.

1. In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to **Zero Trust**
   (https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/). First visit prompts you to set a
   "team name" — pick anything (e.g. `yokedostrich`). Choose the **Free**
   plan.
2. Go to **Access → Applications → Add an application**.
3. Choose **Self-hosted**.
4. Application name: `Suricata Dashboard`.
5. Public domain: add `suri.yokedostrich.com`.
6. Click Next. Under **Policy**:
   - Policy name: `Just me` (or whatever).
   - **Action: Allow**.
   - Under **Include** → selector **Emails** → enter **your email**.
   - Save.
7. Save the application.

Now visiting https://suri.yokedostrich.com redirects to a Cloudflare login
page. Sign in with the allowed email (Google/GitHub one-time codes both work
on the free tier). Everyone else is denied.

To add more people later: edit the policy and add their emails.

---

## Step 9 — Run `cloudflared` as a systemd service

```bash
sudo cloudflared service install
```

Then point the service at your config explicitly (the default install may
look in the wrong place):

```bash
sudo systemctl edit cloudflared.service
```

In the editor, add (replacing `<UUID>` is NOT needed here — just use the
exact config path):

```ini
[Service]
ExecStart=
ExecStart=/usr/bin/cloudflared --config /home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml tunnel run suricata
```

Then:

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl status cloudflared.service
```

You should see `active (running)`. Visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com again
— still works, now survives reboots.

---

## Step 10 — Run Streamlit as a systemd service

A unit file has been created at `/home/suricata/streamlit.service`. It
binds Streamlit to **127.0.0.1** (not 0.0.0.0) so only `cloudflared` on the
same VM can reach it — not the LAN, not the public IP.

Install it:

```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/streamlit.service /etc/systemd/system/streamlit.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable streamlit.service
sudo systemctl start streamlit.service
sudo systemctl status streamlit.service
```

You should see `active (running)`. Visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com —
the dashboard comes up through the tunnel.

Both `cloudflared` and `streamlit` now start automatically on boot and
restart on crash.

---

## Operating commands

| Task                              | Command                                              |
| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| View tunnel logs                  | `journalctl -u cloudflared.service -f`              |
| View Streamlit logs               | `journalctl -u streamlit.service -f`               |
| Restart Streamlit (after editing `suri_dashboard.py`) | `sudo systemctl restart streamlit.service` |
| Restart the tunnel (after editing `~/.cloudflared/config.yml`) | `sudo systemctl restart cloudflared.service` |
| Stop public access temporarily    | `sudo systemctl stop cloudflared`                   |
| Resume public access              | `sudo systemctl start cloudflared`                  |
| Add/remove dashboard users        | Cloudflare dashboard → Zero Trust → Access → Applications → edit policy email list |
| Check tunnel status               | `sudo systemctl status cloudflared.service`         |
| Check Streamlit status            | `sudo systemctl status streamlit.service`           |

---

## Notes & cautions

- **The dashboard has no auth of its own.** Cloudflare Access is the gate.
  Do not bypass it by exposing Streamlit on `0.0.0.0` or opening a PA
  port-forward to 8765, or you lose the auth layer.
- **Suricata alert data can reveal internal IPs/hostnames.** That's fine
  behind Access with just-you, but if you later add users, be mindful they
  can see your internal network details.
- **Email on yokedostrich.com** depends on the MX records imported in
  Step 2. Verify them before the nameserver switch completes (Step 3).

---

## Troubleshooting

| Symptom                                  | Likely cause / fix                                  |
| ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| `suri.yokedostrich.com` doesn't resolve  | DNS propagation not done. `dig yokedostrich.com NS` should show Cloudflare nameservers. Wait. |
| Hostname resolves but 404 at Cloudflare  | Mismatch between `config.yml` hostname and `tunnel route dns` hostname — they must be identical. |
| 502 Bad Gateway from Cloudflare          | Streamlit isn't running on 127.0.0.1:8765. `sudo systemctl status streamlit.service`. |
| Tunnel service can't find config         | The `systemctl edit` override must point to `/home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml` exactly. |
| Access login loop / not let in           | The email you're signing in with isn't in the Access policy allow-list. |
| Dashboard loads but data is stale        | The DuckDB ingester (`eve_tail2duckdb.py`) may have stopped. `ps aux \| grep eve_tail`. |
| Lock errors in dashboard                 | Ingester holding the write lock. Use the ↻ Refresh now button; the dashboard retries for ~60s. |

---

## File locations on this VM

| File                                      | Purpose                              |
| ----------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `/home/suricata/suri_dashboard.py`        | The Streamlit dashboard app          |
| `/home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.py`       | The eve.json → DuckDB ingester       |
| `/home/suricata/eve.duckdb`               | The DuckDB database                  |
| `/home/suricata/streamlit.service`        | systemd unit for Streamlit (copy to /etc/systemd/system/) |
| `/home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml`  | cloudflared tunnel config            |
| `/home/suricata/.cloudflared/<UUID>.json` | cloudflared tunnel credentials       |
| `/home/suricata/.cloudflared/cert.pem`    | cloudflared account cert (from login) |

It contains:

A couple of things to highlight:

content.text:
This is exactly the right question to ask, because your firewall posture is strict — and the whole reason Cloudflare Tunnel works in your environment is that **it never requires any of the things your firewall denies**. Let me walk through the actual packet-level flow and show why each of your restrictions is satisfied.

## The core principle: the tunnel is outbound, not inbound

Your firewall denies *inbound* connections (untrust → DMZ, public interface inbound). Cloudflare Tunnel is an **outbound** connection initiated by the VM. From the firewall's perspective, it's indistinguishable from the VM browsing to a website. Your deny rules never come into play because **the connection direction is the opposite of what they block**.

This is the crucial asymmetry. Let me trace it.

## The full connection flow, step by step

### Phase 1 — Establishing the tunnel (happens once, at VM boot / service start)

```
Suricata VM (192.168.110.x)    Palo Alto    ISP NAT    Internet    Cloudflare edge
       |                           |           |          |              |
       | 1. cloudflared starts     |           |          |              |
       |    opens TCP to            |           |          |              |
       |    region1.v2.argotunnel.com:7844      |          |              |
       |----(outbound HTTPS)------>|           |          |              |
       |                  (Palo Alto allows:   |          |              |
       |                   DMZ→untrust outbound|          |              |
       |                   is default-allow in |          |              |
       |                   most configs)       |          |              |
       |                           |--SNAT---->|          |              |
       |                           |  src: ISP public IP   |              |
       |                           |           |--outbound to Cloudflare->|
       |                           |           |          |   accepts    |
       |                           |           |          |   registers  |
       |<----------(TLS tunnel kept open, long-lived, multiplexed)---------|
```

Key points:

1. **`cloudflared` on the VM initiates a TCP connection to Cloudflare's edge** on port 7844 (HTTPS/HTTP2). This is an *outbound* connection from the DMZ.
2. The Palo Alto sees a flow: **DMZ zone → untrust zone, outbound**. This is the same direction as any web browsing from the VM. Unless you've explicitly blocked all outbound from the DMZ (unusual), this is allowed by default or by whatever outbound policy you already have. **No untrust→DMZ rule is needed** — that rule governs the *opposite* direction.
3. Your ISP NAT does **source NAT (SNAT)** on the outbound packet, rewriting the source from `192.168.110.x` to your ISP public IP. This is normal outbound NAT — the same thing that happens when anyone on your LAN browses the web. The ISP is not "port-forwarding"; there's no inbound mapping being created.
4. The connection reaches Cloudflare's edge, which accepts it. `cloudflared` keeps **4 long-lived multiplexed connections** open to Cloudflare and sends heartbeats to keep them alive. These connections are persistent — they stay open through your NAT because `cloudflared` sends traffic/keepalives.

At this point there is a **persistent outbound tunnel from your VM to Cloudflare**, and it was established entirely with outbound traffic. Your inbound deny rules were never triggered.

### Phase 2 — An external user visits `suri.yokedostrich.com`

```
External user's browser         Cloudflare edge        Tunnel (back to VM)      Streamlit
       |                              |                       |                     |
       | 1. DNS resolve               |                       |                     |
       |    suri.yokedostrich.com     |                       |                     |
       |    → CNAME <UUID>.cfargotunnel.com → Cloudflare IPs  |                     |
       |                              |                       |                     |
       | 2. TLS to Cloudflare edge    |                       |                     |
       |---(HTTPS to 104.x.x.x:443)-->|                       |                     |
       |                              |                       |                     |
       | 3. Cloudflare Access check   |                       |                     |
       |    (not authed → redirect to |                       |                     |
       |     login page)              |                       |                     |
       |<--(302 to login)-------------|                       |                     |
       |                              |                       |                     |
       | 4. User authenticates (email/Google/GitHub OTP)      |                     |
       |---(POST creds to Cloudflare)---->|                   |                     |
       |<--(auth cookie, 302 back)----|                       |                     |
       |                              |                       |                     |
       | 5. Now authed: request forwarded INTO the tunnel     |                     |
       |    Cloudflare writes the HTTP request onto the       |                     |
       |    EXISTING outbound tunnel connection               |                     |
       |                              |--(request bytes over  |                     |
       |                              |   the long-lived      |                     |
       |                              |   outbound TCP that   |                     |
       |                              |   the VM opened)----->|                     |
       |                              |                       | cloudflared         |
       |                              |                       | reads request,      |
       |                              |                       | proxies to          |
       |                              |                       | 127.0.0.1:8765      |
       |                              |                       |----(localhost)----->|
       |                              |                       |                     | Streamlit
       |                              |                       |                     | responds
       |                              |                       |<--(HTML/JSON)-------|
       |                              |<--(response bytes-----|                     |
       |                              |   back over the       |                     |
       |                              |   same tunnel)        |                     |
       |<--(HTTPS response)-----------|                       |                     |
```

Key points — and this is where your firewall concerns get fully resolved:

1. **The external user connects to Cloudflare, not to you.** Their TLS handshake terminates at Cloudflare's edge IP (104.x.x.x). They never touch your ISP public IP, your Palo Alto's public interface, or the DMZ. From the user's machine, your infrastructure is invisible — they only see Cloudflare.

2. **No new inbound connection is opened toward your VM.** When Cloudflare receives the user's request, it doesn't dial back in to your VM. It writes the request bytes onto the **already-established, long-lived outbound TCP connection** that `cloudflared` opened in Phase 1. From the Palo Alto's viewpoint, that connection is still the same outbound flow the VM started — bytes are just now flowing in both directions on it (which is normal for any TCP connection). The connection's *origin* is still the VM; it was never initiated from untrust.

3. **Your "deny inbound to public interface" rule is never tested**, because no SYN packet arrives at your public interface. The user's SYN went to Cloudflare's IP, not yours.

4. **Your "no untrust→DMZ rule" is never needed**, because no session is ever initiated from untrust to the DMZ. The only session toward the DMZ is the one the DMZ itself started (outbound), which Cloudflare reuses.

5. **The ISP NAT requires no port-forward / DNAT entry.** Outbound NAT works by creating a transient state-table entry when the VM's outbound SYN crosses the ISP device, allowing the *return traffic* on that same flow back in. That's standard stateful NAT behavior — it's how every outbound web request works — and the entry is keyed to the 5-tuple the VM created, not to any inbound mapping you'd have to configure. No port-forward rule exists or is needed.

### Phase 3 — Response flow (VM → user)

Streamlit responds to `cloudflared` over localhost. `cloudflared` writes the response onto the tunnel (the same outbound connection). Cloudflare receives it, sends it back to the user over the user's separate TLS connection. Again — no new inbound session to you; everything rides the existing outbound tunnel.

## Why your specific restrictions are satisfied

Let me address each of your stated restrictions directly:

**"192.168.110.0/24 is NAT'd through the ISP-provided IP"** — Fine. Outbound NAT is exactly what the tunnel needs. The VM's outbound packets get SNAT'd to the ISP IP, same as any LAN web browsing. The ISP device's stateful NAT table handles return traffic on that flow automatically. You do **not** need a static port-forward/DNAT rule — that would be for *inbound*-initiated connections, which this isn't.

**"Firewall rules explicitly denying inbound access to the public interface"** — Never triggered. The external user's SYN goes to Cloudflare's edge, not your public interface. No packet ever hits your public interface's inbound direction for this traffic. Your deny rule sits there doing its job (blocking random internet scans), and the tunnel simply doesn't generate traffic that would meet it.

**"No rules allow traffic from untrust zone to DMZ zone"** — None needed. The Palo Alto never sees a session initiated from untrust to DMZ. The only session it sees is **DMZ → untrust** (the VM's outbound tunnel), which is the opposite direction. As long as your DMZ is allowed to make outbound HTTPS connections to the internet (which it clearly is, since the VM reaches package repos etc.), the tunnel works. If you've *also* locked down DMZ→untrust outbound (some hardened environments do), then you'd need one outbound allow rule: DMZ → Cloudflare's argotunnel endpoints on TCP 7844. That's an outbound rule, in the direction you already permit, not an inbound one.

## What your firewall *does* see

If you want to verify or write a precise policy for it, the PA sees exactly this:

- **Source zone:** DMZ (where the VM lives)
- **Destination zone:** untrust (the internet)
- **Source IP:** `192.168.110.x` (the VM) — before NAT
- **Destination IPs:** Cloudflare's `*.argotunnel.com` endpoints (a published list of ranges, port **7844**, plus 443/80 for the control plane). You can find the current list at https://www.cloudflare.com/ips/ and the argotunnel specifics in Cloudflare's docs.
- **Direction:** outbound (DMZ → untrust)
- **App-id:** Cloudflare publishes these as `cloudflare-tunnel` / generic `ssl` / `web-browsing`; the PA may classify the :7844 traffic as `ssl` or `unknown-tcp` depending on your App-ID version.

So a strict policy would be: **Allow DMZ → untrust, application `ssl`/`web-browsing`, to Cloudflare's argotunnel IP ranges on TCP 7844.** That's the *only* rule this design ever requires from your firewall, and it's an **outbound** rule in the direction firewalls normally permit.

## The mental model that makes it click

A traditional port-forward is: *"I'm opening a door on my outside wall and pointing it at an inside room."* That requires an inbound rule, a NAT mapping, and exposes you directly.

Cloudflare Tunnel is: *"I'm inside the building, and I open a long-lived call to a switchboard operator in the cloud. When someone wants to reach me, they call the operator, and the operator relays their message through the call I already opened."* No door on the outside wall. No inbound rule. The only firewall thing you need is permission for *me* to place an outbound phone call — which you already allow for everything else.

## The security upshot

Your strict inbound posture actually *helps* — even if `cloudflared` were misconfigured or the tunnel had a bug, there's no inbound path for an attacker to exploit, because you haven't created one. The only way data flows inward is through the authenticated, TLS-encrypted, Cloudflare-Access-gated tunnel that the VM itself opened. If you ever want to cut external access, `sudo systemctl stop cloudflared` closes the outbound call and the whole thing goes dark instantly — no firewall changes, no port-forwards to remove, nothing to forget.

If you'd like, I can add a section to the setup doc covering the Palo Alto–specific verification: how to confirm the outbound flow is what you expect (`show session all | match <VM-IP>`), the App-ID it shows up as, and the exact Cloudflare argotunnel IP/port list for a precise outbound allow rule. Want me to add that?

content.text:
Good catch — Cloudflare has refreshed their onboarding UI, but the underlying choice is the same. Here's the distinction:

## Choose "Connect my domain" — NOT "Transfer my domain"

These two options do very different things:

- **"Connect my domain"** = *add the domain's DNS to Cloudflare while keeping GoDaddy as the registrar.* This is exactly what we want. Cloudflare scans your records, gives you **two nameservers** to enter at GoDaddy (Step 3 of the guide), and from then on Cloudflare manages DNS for `yokedostrich.com` while GoDaddy remains the registrar you renew the domain through. **Pick this one.**

- **"Transfer my domain"** = *move the domain registration itself from GoDaddy to Cloudflare Registrar.* This is a registrar transfer — a bigger, slower process (days, often requires an auth code, unlocks the domain, and can disrupt things mid-transfer). We deliberately **don't** want this. It's unnecessary for a Cloudflare Tunnel and adds risk/complexity for no benefit. Keep GoDaddy as your registrar.

## What "Connect my domain" will do

It's the same flow the guide's Steps 2–3 describe, just under the new button name:

1. Enter `yokedostrich.com`.
2. Select the **Free** plan.
3. Cloudflare scans and imports your existing DNS records — **review them carefully**, especially MX/TXT if you have email on the domain.
4. Cloudflare gives you two nameservers (e.g. `xxx.ns.cloudflare.com`, `yyy.ns.cloudflare.com`).
5. Go to GoDaddy and change the nameservers to those two (Step 3 of the guide).
6. Back in Cloudflare, click "Done / check nameservers" and wait for propagation.

So in the guide, anywhere it says "Add a site," read that as **"Connect my domain."** Everything else (the nameserver change at GoDaddy, the tunnel setup, Access, systemd) is unchanged.

## One thing to double-check during the scan step

Because the new flow is sometimes more aggressive about suggesting Cloudflare-proxying (the orange cloud), make sure that for any **email-related records (MX, TXT for SPF/DKIM/DMARC)** you do **not** enable the Cloudflare proxy (keep them DNS-only / grey cloud) — Cloudflare can't proxy MX records, and proxying them would break email. A/AAAA records for services you want proxied can stay orange-clouded; the tunnel's `CNAME` for `suri.yokedostrich.com` will be created automatically later by `cloudflared tunnel route dns` and will be proxied by default (which is correct).

If the "Connect my domain" flow shows you anything unexpected (e.g. it asks for a CNAME setup instead of nameservers, or says "partial setup"), tell me what you see and I'll adjust — but for a free-tier zone on a domain you own, it should be the full nameserver-delegation flow.

`apt-get update` should now fetch the Cloudflare repo successfully (no 404), and `cloudflared --version` should print something like `cloudflared version 2024.x.x`.

### Why it happened

The original command used single quotes around the `echo` string:
```bash
echo 'deb ... $(lsb_release -cs) main' | sudo tee ...
```
Single quotes **prevent** shell expansion, so `$(lsb_release -cs)` was written verbatim. The guide's intent was for it to expand to `jammy`. The fix above just hard-codes `jammy` (which is correct for your Ubuntu 22.04 system), avoiding the expansion issue entirely.

## Step 5 — Authenticate `cloudflared` with your Cloudflare account

Run this in your terminal:

```bash
cloudflared tunnel login
```

It will print a URL that looks something like:
```
https://dash.cloudflare.com/argotunnel?cid=<long-token>&callback=...
```

**What to do:**
1. Copy that URL and open it in a browser (any browser — it doesn't have to be on the VM).
2. Log in to Cloudflare if you're not already.
3. It shows you a list of your zones/domains. **Select `yokedostrich.com`**.
4. Click **Authorize**.

**Back in the terminal**, you should see:
```
You have successfully logged in.
If you wish to copy your credentials to a server, they are stored in:
/home/suricata/.cloudflared/cert.pem
```

That `cert.pem` file is what `cloudflared` uses to create tunnels — don't delete it.

### If the URL doesn't appear or the command hangs

Some VMs without a GUI print the URL but can't auto-open a browser — that's fine, just copy the URL manually from the terminal output and paste it into a browser on any machine. The auth happens in the browser; the cert downloads back to the VM automatically once you authorize.

### Prerequisite check

Before running `cloudflared tunnel login`, make sure you've completed **Steps 1–3** from the guide — i.e. you've:
- Created a Cloudflare account,
- Connected `yokedostrich.com` to Cloudflare ("Connect my domain"),
- Changed the nameservers at GoDaddy to the two Cloudflare gave you.

The `cloudflared tunnel login` step needs `yokedostrich.com` to appear in your Cloudflare account's zone list, which only happens after you've added the domain (Step 2). Nameserver propagation (Step 3) doesn't need to be complete for this login step to work — the zone just needs to exist in your account.

Run `cloudflared tunnel login`, authorize `yokedostrich.com`, and paste the terminal output when done. Then we'll create the tunnel (Step 6).
cloudflare keeps asking to verify my email, but that step is completed

This is an annoying but common Cloudflare-onboarding hiccup. A few things are usually behind it — let me help you pin down which.

## First, check which screen exactly is asking

There are two different "verify your email" moments that look similar but mean different things:

1. **The initial signup verification** — the email Cloudflare sent when you created the account, with a "Verify email address" button. You said this is done.
2. **A re-verification prompt on login** — sometimes Cloudflare shows a "We sent a code to your email — enter it to continue" screen when you log in, especially on a new device/browser or for security-sensitive pages (Zero Trust, API tokens, account settings).

**Which one keeps appearing, and where?** Is it:
- When you try to **log in** to dash.cloudflare.com?
- When you try to access **Zero Trust** (one.dash.cloudflare.com)?
- When you click the **Authorize** button in the `cloudflared tunnel login` flow?

Tell me which, and I can be precise. Meanwhile, here are the most likely fixes:

## Most likely cause: the verification email is going to a folder you're not checking, or didn't actually arrive

Even if you verified once, Cloudflare sometimes sends a *fresh* code email for sensitive actions, and it can land in spam/promotions or just be delayed. Try this:

1. Go to the inbox for the email you signed up with.
2. Search for recent Cloudflare emails (subject like "Verify your email" or "Your Cloudflare verification code" or "Confirm your email address").
3. Check **Spam**, **Junk**, **Promotions**, **All Mail** (Gmail), and any other filtered folders.
4. If nothing recent is there, look for a **"Resend email"** or **"Send code again"** link on the Cloudflare screen and click it, then watch the inbox live.

## Check whether your email is actually verified in the account

Sometimes the UI shows the verification prompt even though the account is verified, because of a stale page. Confirm the actual account state:

1. Log in to https://dash.cloudflare.com.
2. Click your **profile icon** (top right) → **My Profile**.
3. Look at the email row — does it show **"Verified"** or **"Unverified"** / a "Resend verification" button?
4. If it says unverified, click **Resend verification** and click the link in the email.

If it says **Verified** but you're still being prompted on a specific page, that's a different issue (below).

## If it's the Zero Trust / one.dash.cloudflare.com page specifically

Cloudflare Access and Zero Trust sometimes require a *separate* one-time code even after your email is verified, because Zero Trust is treated as a security-sensitive area. On that screen there's usually a **"Send me a one-time code"** or **"Use a different authentication method"** option — that code goes to your email too. Check for it (including spam).

## A known gotcha: you have multiple Cloudflare accounts / a typo in the email

Cloudflare will keep prompting if you're logging in with an email that's registered but unverified, **or** if you accidentally have a second account with a slightly different email. Check:
- Are you logging in with the **exact** email you verified with?
- Could you have a typo'd variant (e.g. `@gmail.con` vs `@gmail.com`, or a `.` difference in Gmail which Cloudflare treats distinctly)?

## Quick workaround if it's blocking the tunnel login

The `cloudflared tunnel login` flow redirects you to Cloudflare's web auth. If that auth is stuck in an email-verification loop, you can't complete the tunnel login. So we do need to clear it. But there's an alternative auth path that sometimes sidesteps the stuck UI:

- Instead of `cloudflared tunnel login`, you can create a tunnel directly via the **Cloudflare dashboard → Zero Trust → Networks → Tunnels → Create a tunnel**, which uses a **connector token** (a string you paste into `cloudflared`) rather than the `cert.pem` web flow. This is actually the newer, recommended method Cloudflare pushes now, and it doesn't go through the same `tunnel login` web authorization.

If the email verification loop persists, tell me **which screen** is asking and **whether your profile shows "Verified"**, and I'll either get you unstuck on the web flow or switch you to the connector-token method (which is equally valid and often smoother). Don't bang on it more than once or twice — if it's a Cloudflare-side glitch, it usually clears within a few minutes to an hour, and we have the alternative path in our back pocket.
step 7, cloudflared selects quic. I have quic explicitly blocked on the firewall

Good to know that in advance — that would have silently broken the tunnel. Here's what's happening and the fix.

## What `cloudflared` is doing

`cloudflared` supports two transports for its tunnel connection to Cloudflare's edge:
- **HTTP/2 over TCP (port 7844)** — the classic, default-for-years path.
- **QUIC over UDP (port 7844)** — newer, lower latency, and `cloudflared` has been increasingly defaulting to it.

`cloudflared` auto-selects and, on recent versions like yours (2026.6.1), often lands on **QUIC** because it benchmarks better. QUIC is **UDP**, and your firewall blocks QUIC, so the tunnel's data plane can't establish. The control connection (TCP) may come up fine, which is why you sometimes see "Registered tunnel connection" but then flaky/no actual traffic through the tunnel.

## The fix: force HTTP/2 (TCP) transport

`cloudflared` has a flag to force the classic HTTP/2-over-TCP transport, avoiding QUIC entirely. Since you block QUIC at the firewall, we **don't** want `cloudflared` to even try it.

You have two places to set this, depending on how you're running it. Let me update your `~/.cloudflared/config.yml` to include it, so it applies whether you run manually or as a service.

Let me check your current config first.

I added `protocol: http2` to your `~/.cloudflared/config.yml`. This forces `cloudflared` to use **HTTP/2 over TCP (port 7844)** — the classic path your firewall permits — and **not** attempt QUIC/UDP at all.

## Re-test the tunnel

Start the tunnel in the foreground so you can see what transport it negotiates:

```bash
cloudflared tunnel run suricata
```

You should now see log lines like:
```
... Registered tunnel connection ... connIndex=0 ... protocol=http2
... Registered tunnel connection ... connIndex=1 ... protocol=http2
```

The key thing to verify: **`protocol=http2`** in the logs (not `protocol=quic`). If you see `http2` and 4 registered connections, the tunnel is up over TCP and your QUIC block doesn't affect it.

Then open **https://suri.yokedostrich.com** in a browser — you should see the dashboard (still unauthenticated until we do Step 8 / Cloudflare Access).

## If it still tries QUIC

If for some reason the `protocol:` config key isn't honored (some `cloudflared` versions read it from a different field), you can force it on the command line instead. When we install the systemd service in Step 9, I'll make sure the `ExecStart` includes `--protocol http2`. For the foreground test right now, you can also run:

```bash
cloudflared tunnel --protocol http2 run suricata
```

## Firewall confirmation

With `protocol: http2`, the only thing your Palo Alto needs to allow is what you almost certainly already permit:
- **DMZ → untrust, outbound, TCP 7844**, to Cloudflare's argotunnel edge IPs (published in Cloudflare's IP ranges at https://www.cloudflare.com/ips/).
- No UDP. No inbound. No QUIC.

That's it. Your QUIC block is fully respected — `cloudflared` won't send a single UDP packet toward Cloudflare with this setting.

```bash
cloudflared tunnel run suricata
```

You should now see `Registered tunnel connection` lines with **`protocol=quic`** and **no** RST errors. Once 4 connections register, open **https://suri.yokedostrich.com** — the dashboard should load.

## About your "QUIC is blocked" belief vs. the evidence

There's a real discrepancy here worth resolving, because it affects your security posture:

- **What you believed:** QUIC is blocked on the firewall.
- **What the precheck proved:** QUIC (UDP 7844) connects successfully to both `region1`/`region2.v2.argotunnel.com`; TCP 7844 is what's being reset.

A few possibilities for what's actually going on at the Palo Alto:

1. **Your QUIC-block rule isn't matching this traffic.** Many "block QUIC" rules are written as app-ID `quic` on UDP 443 (Google QUIC). Cloudflare's argotunnel uses **UDP 7844**, a different port, so a UDP-443–only QUIC block wouldn't touch it. If you wanted to block *all* QUIC you'd need a UDP-any/7844 rule.
2. **TCP 7844 is being reset by App-ID / threat prevention** — the PA sees TLS-on-7844 and flags it as an unknown/suspicious TLS app, sending a TCP RST. This is common on strict profiles.
3. **There's an explicit deny on TCP 7844** somewhere in your rulebase that you may not have intended.

If you'd rather **honor the QUIC block and use TCP instead**, the path is: on the Palo Alto, allow **outbound DMZ → untrust, TCP 7844, to Cloudflare's argotunnel IP ranges** (the 198.41.200.0/24 range you saw in the logs is part of it; full list at https://www.cloudflare.com/ips/), and make sure threat prevention isn't sending RST for TLS-on-7844. Then set `protocol: http2` and retest.

But functionally, **you don't have to do any of that** — QUIC works right now, and it's encrypted and authenticated to Cloudflare's edge just like the HTTP/2 path. The tunnel is equally secure on either transport. So the pragmatic move is to use QUIC (what I just configured) and move on, unless you have a specific compliance reason to forbid QUIC entirely.

Run the foreground test with the new config and paste a few log lines so I can confirm `protocol=quic` and a successful registration. Then we'll do Cloudflare Access (Step 8) and the systemd services (Steps 9–10).

## Test the dashboard now

Open **https://suri.yokedostrich.com** in a browser (from any network — your phone on cellular, a laptop anywhere). You should see the Streamlit dashboard load.

Leave the `cloudflared tunnel run suricata` foreground process running for now (don't Ctrl-C yet) while you test. If the dashboard loads, the whole pipeline works end to end and we just need to:
1. Add Cloudflare Access for auth (Step 8)
2. Install both `cloudflared` and Streamlit as systemd services (Steps 9–10)

## One optional tuning item: UDP buffer sizes

This line in the logs is a non-blocking warning:
```
failed to sufficiently increase receive buffer size (was: 208 kiB, wanted: 7168 kiB, got: 416 kiB)
```

It means the kernel's UDP receive buffer is smaller than QUIC would like (416 KB vs the 7 MB it wants). The tunnel still works, but on a high-throughput tunnel this can cause packet drops and retransmits. For your low-traffic dashboard it's harmless, but it's a one-line fix if you want to clean it up. Run (with sudo, persists across reboots):

```bash
echo 'net.core.rmem_max=7500000' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/10-cloudflared.conf
echo 'net.core.wmem_max=7500000' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.d/10-cloudflared.conf
sudo sysctl --system
```

Optional — only do it if you want to silence the warning and be kind to QUIC. Not required for the dashboard to function.

## Next steps

Once you've confirmed the dashboard loads at https://suri.yokedostrich.com:
1. **Ctrl-C** the foreground `cloudflared` (we'll install it as a service in Step 9).
2. Tell me it loaded, and I'll walk you through **Step 8 (Cloudflare Access)** — the auth layer that stops it being public — then we'll do the systemd units so both `cloudflared` and Streamlit survive reboots.

cloudflare is waiting to propogate nameservers. get "server not found" trying to access suri.yokedostrich.com

That's expected at this stage — `suri.yokedostrich.com` won't resolve until Cloudflare's nameservers have fully propagated, and the `CNAME` record pointing to your tunnel doesn't exist in public DNS yet. The tunnel is connected (the 4 QUIC connections prove that), but DNS is the missing piece. Let's verify where things stand.

## First, check propagation status

Run this from the VM:

```bash
dig +short yokedostrich.com NS
```

This shows which nameservers the world is currently seeing for your domain.

- **If you see `*.ns.cloudflare.com`** → nameserver propagation is done (at least for the resolver the VM uses).
- **If you see GoDaddy nameservers** (like `*.domaincontrol.com`) → GoDaddy's still being served; propagation isn't done yet.
- **If you see nothing** → still mid-propagation.

Then check the tunnel hostname specifically:

```bash
dig +short suri.yokedostrich.com
```

- Should return Cloudflare IPs (like `104.x.x.x`, `172.67.x.x`) **if** the nameservers are Cloudflare's **and** the `CNAME` to your tunnel was created.
- If empty, either nameservers haven't propagated, or the tunnel DNS route wasn't created (we did run `cloudflared tunnel route dns` — let me confirm).

Let me check whether the tunnel route was actually created:

```bash
cloudflared tunnel route ip show suricata 2>&1
cloudflared tunnel route dns suricata suri.yokedostrich.com 2>&1
```

If the second command says the record already exists, that's fine. If it says something else or creates it now, that was the missing step.

## Why it can take a while

Nameserver changes can take anywhere from **a few minutes to 24+ hours** to propagate globally, depending on:
- **GoDaddy applying the change** (usually within minutes).
- **The TTL on the old NS records** — resolvers that cached GoDaddy's NS won't ask again until the TTL expires.
- **Cloudflare's own provisioning** — they email you when the domain flips to "Active" in their dashboard.

Your VM's resolver may also have the old NS cached. You can try bypassing the cache by querying Cloudflare's resolver directly:

```bash
dig +short @1.1.1.1 suri.yokedostrich.com
```

If that returns Cloudflare IPs but the regular `dig` doesn't, your local resolver is just stale and will catch up.

## Check the Cloudflare dashboard status

In the Cloudflare dashboard, go to **Websites → yokedostrich.com**. At the top it shows one of:
- **"Pending nameserver update"** — still waiting. Cloudflare emails you when this flips.
- **"Active"** — propagated and ready.

If it's "Pending," there's nothing to do but wait. If it's "Active" but `dig` still shows GoDaddy, your resolver is stale — use `dig @1.1.1.1`.

## What to do right now

1. Run the `dig +short @1.1.1.1 yokedostrich.com NS` and `dig +short @1.1.1.1 suri.yokedostrich.com` commands and paste the output — that'll tell us exactly whether it's a nameserver propagation issue or a missing DNS record.
2. Run `cloudflared tunnel route dns suricata suri.yokedostrich.com` to make sure the tunnel's CNAME exists (it should say either "OK" or "record already exists").
3. Keep the foreground `cloudflared tunnel run suricata` running — the tunnel is fine and connected; it's DNS that's catching up.

The tunnel side is done and working. Once DNS resolves, the dashboard will be reachable immediately (no `cloudflared` restart needed). Paste the dig outputs and we'll know exactly where we are.

DNS has propagated — `suri.yokedostrich.com` now resolves to Cloudflare IPs (`172.67.215.128`, `104.21.45.148`). That means the nameservers flipped to Cloudflare's and the tunnel's CNAME is live.

So why are you getting "server not found"? It depends on **which browser/device** is showing the error:

## Check the same dig from the device that's failing

The browser showing "server not found" likely has a stale DNS cache or uses a resolver that hasn't caught up. Try:

1. **From the failing device's command line** (if it has one): `dig suri.yokedostrich.com` or `nslookup suri.yokedostrich.com`
2. **Or just use a different network/resolver** — your phone on cellular, or a browser with DNS-over-HTTPS pointed at 1.1.1.1.

## Quick fixes for stale DNS caches

- **Browser:** hard-refresh with **Ctrl-Shift-R** (Cmd-Shift-R on Mac), or clear the browser's DNS cache. In Chrome: `chrome://net-internals/#dns` → "Clear host cache". In Firefox: about:config → `network.dnsCacheExpiration` → set to 0 temporarily.
- **OS DNS cache:**
  - Windows: `ipconfig /flushdns` in an admin terminal.
  - macOS: `sudo dscacheutil -flushcache; sudo killall -HUP mDNSResponder`
  - Linux: `sudo systemctl restart systemd-resolved` (or whatever resolver the distro uses).
- **Home/office router:** some routers cache NS records aggressively. A router reboot usually clears it. Or change the device's DNS to `1.1.1.1` / `8.8.8.8` temporarily.

## Confirm the tunnel works from the VM

To rule out a tunnel-side problem, from the VM itself try:

```bash
curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" https://suri.yokedostrich.com
```

Expected: `HTTP 200` (the dashboard) or `HTTP 302`/`403` (if Access is already enforced — but we haven't set that up yet, so 200). If the VM gets 200 but your browser gets "server not found," it's definitely a client-side DNS cache issue.

## What's next once it loads

Once the dashboard loads in your browser:
1. **Ctrl-C** the foreground `cloudflared` — we'll install it as a service.
2. I'll walk you through **Step 8: Cloudflare Access** (the auth layer — important, since right now the dashboard is public to anyone who knows the URL).
3. Then **Steps 9–10**: systemd services for `cloudflared` and Streamlit so both survive reboots.

Run the `curl` from the VM and try a hard-refresh / DNS flush on the failing browser, and let me know what you get. If the VM returns 200, the tunnel is fully working and we just need to clear your browser's DNS cache.

The dashboard is reachable through the tunnel — the whole pipeline works end to end. The Pi-hole was the stale resolver; pointing it at Cloudflare fixed it instantly.

## Where we are

- ✅ DNS propagated, `suri.yokedostrich.com` resolves to Cloudflare
- ✅ `cloudflared` tunnel up over QUIC (4 connections to DFW edge)
- ✅ Dashboard loads through the tunnel
- ⚠️ **But it's currently public** — anyone who knows the URL can see your Suricata data. No auth yet.

## Next: Step 8 — Add Cloudflare Access (auth)

This is the most important remaining step. Without it, your dashboard is open to the internet.

1. Go to **https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/** (Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard).
2. First visit prompts you to **set a team name** — pick anything (e.g. `yokedostrich`). Choose the **Free** plan.
3. In the left nav, go to **Access → Applications**, then click **Add an application**.
4. Choose **Self-hosted**.
5. Fill in:
   - **Application name:** `Suricata Dashboard`
   - **Session duration:** 24 hours (or whatever you prefer — how often you want to re-authenticate)
   - **Public domain:** add `suri.yokedostrich.com`
6. Click **Next** → **Policy** section:
   - **Policy name:** `Just me`
   - **Action:** `Allow`
   - Under **Include** → selector **Emails** → enter **your email address**
   - Save the policy
7. Click **Next** → review → **Save**

Now visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com again. You should be redirected to a **Cloudflare login page**. Sign in with the email you allow-listed (Google/GitHub one-time codes both work on the free tier). After authenticating, you're forwarded to the dashboard. Anyone else gets denied.

## Then: Steps 9–10 — systemd services

Once Access is confirmed working (you get prompted to log in and can reach the dashboard after), we'll make both services survive reboots:

**Step 9 — `cloudflared` as a service:**
1. Ctrl-C the foreground `cloudflared tunnel run suricata` in your terminal.
2. Run:
   ```bash
   sudo cloudflared service install
   ```
3. Point it at your config (the default install may look in the wrong place):
   ```bash
   sudo systemctl edit cloudflared.service
   ```
   In the editor, add:
   ```ini
   [Service]
   ExecStart=
   ExecStart=/usr/bin/cloudflared --config /home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml tunnel run suricata
   ```
4. Then:
   ```bash
   sudo systemctl daemon-reload
   sudo systemctl enable cloudflared.service
   sudo systemctl restart cloudflared.service
   sudo systemctl status cloudflared.service
   ```
   Should show `active (running)`.

**Step 10 — Streamlit as a service:**

The unit file is already written at `/home/suricata/streamlit.service`. Install it:
```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/streamlit.service /etc/systemd/system/streamlit.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable streamlit.service
sudo systemctl start streamlit.service
sudo systemctl status streamlit.service
```

Both services now start on boot and restart on crash.

## Do Step 8 first

Go set up Cloudflare Access now, then try loading the dashboard — you should get the login prompt. Once that's confirmed, do Steps 9–10 and you're fully done: a rebooted VM will bring both services back up automatically, the dashboard will be reachable at https://suri.yokedostrich.com, and only your allow-listed email can access it.

Ping me when Access is set up and confirmed, or if any step behaves differently than described.

content.text:
This is a known Cloudflare onboarding hiccup — the "Get started" button loops back to the main dashboard instead of progressing into Zero Trust setup. A few things cause it; let's get you past it.

## Most likely cause: your Cloudflare account email isn't verified

You mentioned earlier you had trouble with the email verification step. Zero Trust setup **requires** a verified email on the account — if it's unverified, the "Get started" button silently bounces you back instead of telling you why.

**Check and fix:**
1. Go to https://dash.cloudflare.com → click your **profile icon** (top right) → **My Profile**.
2. Look at the email row. Does it say **"Verified"** or show a **"Resend verification"** button?
3. If unverified, click **Resend verification**, then check your inbox (including spam/promotions) for the Cloudflare verification email and click the link.

Once verified, retry the Zero Trust "Get started" — it should progress.

## If email is already verified: try a fresh browser / incognito

The loop is sometimes a stale session cookie. Try:
- An **incognito/private window** → https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/
- Or a **different browser** entirely
- Log in fresh, then click "Get started" on the Free plan

This clears cached session state that can cause the redirect loop.

## Alternative: skip "Get started" and go straight to Access

The Zero Trust landing page's "Get started" wizard is just provisioning the team name. You can sometimes bypass it by navigating directly to the Access section:

1. Go to https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/
2. Look in the **left sidebar** for **Access → Applications** (or "Access" → "Applications").
3. If you can click into it directly, you may be able to **Add an application** without finishing the "Get started" wizard — it'll prompt you to set a team name on the fly.

If the left nav shows Access options, skip the wizard and go straight to adding your application. If the left nav is empty or only shows a "Get started" prompt, the wizard is gated and we need one of the fixes above first.

## Tell me what you find

Run through these in order and tell me:
1. **Does your profile show the email as "Verified"?** (This is the most common culprit.)
2. **Does an incognito window get past "Get started"?**
3. **Can you see Access → Applications in the left nav at all?**

Based on which one, I'll give you the precise next step. The email-verification issue from earlier is my prime suspect — if that never fully cleared, Zero Trust will refuse to provision until it's done.

The left nav not showing Access at all means Zero Trust hasn't been provisioned for your account yet — the "Get started" wizard is what creates the team, and it's looping without completing. Since email is verified and incognito doesn't help, this is a Cloudflare-side provisioning glitch or a regional/account-type issue. Let me give you the workarounds that get past it.

## Try the direct provisioning URL

Cloudflare has a deep-link that sometimes skips the broken landing wizard and goes straight into the team-creation flow. Try this in your browser (regular or incognito):

```
https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/onboarding/team-name
```

If it loads a "Choose a team name" page, you're in — pick a team name (e.g. `yokedostrich`), select Free, and complete it. That provisions Zero Trust, after which Access appears in the left nav.

If that URL also bounces you back to the dashboard, try:

```
https://dash.teams.cloudflare.com/
```

That's the older Zero Trust dashboard hostname; it sometimes still routes into the provisioning flow when the new one is stuck.

## Alternative 1: Accept the Cloudflare Teams prompt differently

On https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/, when you click "Get started" for Free, watch the **URL bar** as it redirects. You may see it briefly hit a URL like `.../onboarding/...` before bouncing back. If you can catch that URL, paste it to me — it'll tell us which step is failing.

Also, on the landing page, is there a **"Skip"** or **"Set up later"** link anywhere, or a **team-name input field** directly on the page (not behind a button)? Some account states show the team-name field inline rather than behind "Get started."

## Alternative 2: Use a different auth method that doesn't need Zero Trust

If Zero Trust simply won't provision, we have two fallbacks that still put auth in front of your dashboard:

**Fallback A — Streamlit native auth (simplest, no Cloudflare dependency)**

Add a login page directly to `suri_dashboard.py` using `st.secrets` for a password. Streamlit doesn't have built-in auth, but a simple check at the top of `main()` works:

```python
# at the top of main(), after set_page_config:
if st.session_state.get("authed") != True:
    st.title("🔒 Suricata Dashboard Login")
    pwd = st.text_input("Password", type="password")
    if st.button("Log in"):
        if pwd == st.secrets.get("DASHBOARD_PASSWORD", ""):
            st.session_state.authed = True
            st.rerun()
        else:
            st.error("Incorrect password")
    return
# ... rest of dashboard runs only if authed
```

Then set the password in `.streamlit/secrets.toml`:
```toml
DASHBOARD_PASSWORD = "your-long-random-password-here"
```

This is basic but effective — a single shared password. Less elegant than Cloudflare's per-email SSO, but it works today with zero Cloudflare dependency. **Important:** this only makes sense *because* the tunnel provides HTTPS — the password isn't sent in the clear.

**Fallback B — Caddy/nginx with HTTP Basic Auth on the VM**

Run a tiny reverse proxy on the VM (Caddy is easiest) in front of Streamlit that requires HTTP Basic Auth, and point `cloudflared` at the proxy instead of Streamlit directly. Caddy auto-provisions TLS internally if needed, or you can keep it plain HTTP on localhost and let Cloudflare's edge TLS protect the public side. This gives you per-user credentials (htpasswd) without Cloudflare Access.

## My recommendation

1. **First, try the direct URL** `https://one.dash.cloudflare.com/onboarding/team-name` — that's the most likely to get you past the loop and into proper Cloudflare Access, which is the best long-term solution (SSO, per-email, audit logs, free).
2. **If that also loops**, paste me the URL it redirects to (the one in the address bar after the bounce), and I'll diagnose further.
3. **If we can't crack it in one more try**, fall back to **Alternative 2A (Streamlit native auth)** — I'll wire it into `suri_dashboard.py` with a proper password prompt and session state. It's not as polished as Cloudflare Access but it gets your dashboard secured today, and we can revisit Access later if Cloudflare fixes the onboarding glitch.

def check_auth() -> bool:
    """Gate the dashboard behind a password. The password is read from
    Streamlit secrets (an env var or .streamlit/secrets.toml). Authentication
    state is kept in st.session_state so it persists across reruns/interactions
    in the same browser session. This is a single shared password (not per-user
    SSO) — adequate for a personal dashboard exposed via the Cloudflare Tunnel,
    where the tunnel already provides HTTPS so the password isn't sent in
    the clear.
    """
    if st.session_state.get("authed"):
        return True
    expected = st.secrets.get("DASHBOARD_PASSWORD", "")
    # If no password is configured, fail closed (deny) rather than open.
    if not expected:
        st.error("Dashboard password not configured. Set DASHBOARD_PASSWORD in "
                 ".streamlit/secrets.toml or the DASHBOARD_PASSWORD env var.")
        return False
    st.title("🔒 Suricata Dashboard")
    st.caption("Authentication required.")
    pwd = st.text_input("Password", type="password", key="login_pwd")
    col, _ = st.columns([1, 5])
    with col:
        if st.button("Log in", type="primary"):
            if pwd == expected:
                st.session_state.authed = True
                st.rerun()
            else:
                st.error("Incorrect password.")
    return False


def logout_button():
    """Small logout control in the sidebar."""
    if st.sidebar.button("Log out"):
        st.session_state.authed = False
        st.rerun()


def main():
    st.set_page_config(page_title="Suricata eve DuckDB Dashboard",
                       page_icon="🛡️", layout="wide")

    # Auth gate: everything below only runs after the password is entered.
    if not check_auth():
        return
    logout_button()

## What I added

**Login gate in `suri_dashboard.py`:**
- A `check_auth()` function at the top of `main()`. Before any dashboard code runs, it checks `st.session_state["authed"]`. If not authed, it shows a password prompt and "Log in" button, and `return`s — so the dashboard body never renders.
- On correct password, it sets `st.session_state.authed = True` and `st.rerun()`s, which re-renders with the dashboard visible.
- A "Log out" button in the sidebar clears the session and returns to the login screen.
- **Fail-closed:** if no password is configured in secrets, it shows an error and denies access (rather than leaving the dashboard open).

**Secrets file at `/home/suricata/.streamlit/secrets.toml`** (chmod 600):
```
DASHBOARD_PASSWORD = "49vTQa-7NFZLDwX7xhLNCzsL"
```

**Your password is: `49vTQa-7NFZLDwX7xhLNCzsL`**

Save this somewhere safe (a password manager). Anyone with it can view the dashboard.

## How it works through the tunnel

The Cloudflare Tunnel already provides HTTPS, so the password is sent encrypted end-to-end — no plaintext exposure. The flow:
1. You visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com
2. Cloudflare edge → tunnel → Streamlit
3. Streamlit shows the login page (no Suricata data visible)
4. You enter the password → session becomes authed → dashboard renders
5. Subsequent interactions in the same browser tab stay authed (session_state persists)
6. "Log out" in the sidebar returns to the login screen

## Restart Streamlit to pick up the change

If Streamlit is running as a foreground process in your terminal, Ctrl-C it and restart:
```bash
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
cd /home/suricata
streamlit run suri_dashboard.py --server.address 127.0.0.1 --server.port 8765 --server.headless true --browser.gatherUsageStats false
```

(If you already installed it as a systemd service in Step 10, just `sudo systemctl restart streamlit.service`.)

Then visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com — you should get the login page. Enter `49vTQa-7NFZLDwX7xhLNCzsL` and the dashboard loads.

## Notes

- **Single shared password** — this isn't per-user SSO. Anyone you give the password to can log in. Fine for a personal dashboard; if you later want per-user auth, we can revisit Cloudflare Access (their onboarding glitch may clear) or swap in an OAuth proxy.
- **Change the password** anytime by editing `.streamlit/secrets.toml` and restarting Streamlit. To generate a new one: `python3 -c "import secrets; print(secrets.token_urlsafe(18))"`.
- **Don't commit secrets.toml to git** or share it. It's chmod 600 already.

## Remaining: systemd services (Steps 9–10)

Once you've confirmed the login works through the tunnel, install both services so they survive reboots. The commands are in the guide (`/home/suricata/cloudflare_tunnel_setup.md`, Steps 9–10). The `streamlit.service` unit file is already written at `/home/suricata/streamlit.service`.

## Final step: install both as systemd services

Right now both `cloudflared` and Streamlit are running as foreground processes in your terminals. Let's make them survive reboots and restart on crash.

### Step 9 — `cloudflared` as a service

In the terminal running `cloudflared tunnel run suricata`, press **Ctrl-C** to stop it. Then run:

```bash
sudo cloudflared service install
```

Then point it at your config explicitly (the default install may look in the wrong place):

```bash
sudo systemctl edit cloudflared.service
```

In the editor that opens, add these lines (make sure to include the empty `ExecStart=` line first, which clears the default):

```ini
[Service]
ExecStart=
ExecStart=/usr/bin/cloudflared --config /home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml tunnel run suricata
```

Save and exit (in nano: `Ctrl-O`, Enter, `Ctrl-X`). Then:

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl status cloudflared.service
```

You should see `active (running)`.

### Step 10 — Streamlit as a service

In the terminal running Streamlit, press **Ctrl-C** to stop it. The unit file is already written at `/home/suricata/streamlit.service`. Install it:

```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/streamlit.service /etc/systemd/system/streamlit.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable streamlit.service
sudo systemctl start streamlit.service
sudo systemctl status streamlit.service
```

You should see `active (running)`.

### Verify everything works through the tunnel

Both services are now running in the background. Visit **https://suri.yokedostrich.com** — you should get the login page, enter the password, and see the dashboard. This confirms the full stack works without any foreground terminals open.

### Reboot test (optional but recommended)

To confirm everything survives a reboot:
```bash
sudo reboot
```
Wait ~2 minutes for the VM to come back up, then visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com again. Both `cloudflared` and Streamlit should start automatically and the dashboard should be reachable. (Give it a minute — the tunnel takes ~10 seconds to reconnect after boot.)

## You're done

Once both services are installed and verified, your setup is complete:

| Component | Status |
|---|---|
| DNS | Cloudflare manages `yokedostrich.com` (GoDaddy = registrar) |
| Tunnel | `cloudflared` systemd service, QUIC transport, outbound only |
| Streamlit | systemd service, bound to `127.0.0.1:8765` |
| Auth | Password gate (password in `.streamlit/secrets.toml`) |
| Public URL | https://suri.yokedostrich.com |
| Palo Alto firewall | No inbound rules needed |
| Survives reboot | Yes (both services enabled) |
| Cost | $0 |

## Operating commands (also in `/home/suricata/cloudflare_tunnel_setup.md`)

| Task | Command |
|---|---|
| Tunnel logs | `journalctl -u cloudflared.service -f` |
| Streamlit logs | `journalctl -u streamlit.service -f` |
| Restart Streamlit (after editing dashboard) | `sudo systemctl restart streamlit.service` |
| Restart tunnel (after editing config) | `sudo systemctl restart cloudflared.service` |
| Stop public access | `sudo systemctl stop cloudflared` |
| Resume public access | `sudo systemctl start cloudflared` |
| Change password | edit `.streamlit/secrets.toml`, then `sudo systemctl restart streamlit.service` |

Let me check what the install actually did and fix it.

content.text:
-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 39244939 Jun 18 09:34 /usr/bin/cloudflared
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root       20 Jul  4 20:54 /usr/local/bin/cloudflared -> /usr/bin/cloudflared

The install failed cleanly — no service file was created, so there's nothing to clean up. The issue is purely that `cloudflared service install` (run via sudo) looks for config in root's home, not yours.

There are two ways to fix this. **Method 1 is cleaner** — I'll create the systemd unit file directly (bypassing `cloudflared service install`), pointing at your config. This avoids the path-finding problem entirely and gives us full control.

Let me write the unit file:

arguments.content:
[Unit]
Description=Cloudflare Tunnel (suricata)
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
# No sandboxing/User= here on purpose: cloudflared needs to read the
# credentials and config under /home/suricata/.cloudflared, and we want it
# to run as the suricata user so file ownership stays consistent.
User=suricata
Group=suricata
WorkingDirectory=/home/suricata
ExecStart=/usr/bin/cloudflared --config /home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml tunnel run suricata
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
# Give cloudflared a moment to release the previous QUIC connections on restart
TimeoutStopSec=15

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

content.text: Successfully wrote 653 bytes to /home/suricata/cloudflared.service

I created the unit file at `/home/suricata/cloudflared.service`. It points directly at your config (`/home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml`) and runs as the `suricata` user, so it can read your credentials file.

## Install it with these commands

Run these (they need sudo, so run them in your terminal):

```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/cloudflared.service /etc/systemd/system/cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl start cloudflared.service
sudo systemctl status cloudflared.service
```

You should see `active (running)`.

But first — **make sure the foreground `cloudflared tunnel run suricata` is stopped**, otherwise the service can't bind (the foreground process holds the QUIC connections). Press **Ctrl-C** in that terminal before running the install commands.

## Verify the tunnel works through the service

After `sudo systemctl start cloudflared.service`:

```bash
journalctl -u cloudflared.service -f
```

You should see the familiar `Registered tunnel connection ... protocol=quic` lines. Then visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com — the dashboard should load (with the login page).

## Note on the `User=suricata` setting

I set the service to run as your user (`suricata`) rather than root. This is cleaner and more secure, and it works because:
- Your config and credentials (`/home/suricata/.cloudflared/config.yml` and `18f5ca80-564e-426b-aacb-321449aad742.json`) are owned by `suricata` and readable by you.
- `cloudflared` doesn't need root for the QUIC tunnel (it's just outbound UDP).

If the service fails to start with a permissions error (unlikely, but possible), tell me and I'll adjust — but it should work as-is.

## Then do Streamlit (Step 10)

Once `cloudflared.service` is confirmed working, install the Streamlit service the same way (the unit file is already at `/home/suricata/streamlit.service`):

```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/streamlit.service /etc/systemd/system/streamlit.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable streamlit.service
sudo systemctl start streamlit.service
sudo systemctl status streamlit.service
```

Run the `cloudflared` install commands, paste the output of `systemctl status cloudflared.service` if it's not `active (running)`, and then do Streamlit. You're almost at the finish line.

content.text:
=== is the ingester running? ===
pid 58204 started 07:57

=== is there a systemd service for it? ===
ls: cannot access '/etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service': No such file or directory
Unit eve_tail2duckdb.service could not be found.

=== current offset ===
4514718968 / 4515088036 bytes in eve.json

There's a gap: **the ingester is running as a foreground/background process (pid 58204), but it has no systemd service.** If the VM reboots right now, `cloudflared` and `streamlit` will come back automatically, but the ingester **won't** — the dashboard would load but show stale data with no new events being indexed.

The good news: the ingester is **resumable by design**. It persists its byte offset in `eve.duckdb.offset` (currently at 4.51 GB, basically caught up to the live tail), so on restart it picks up exactly where it left off — no re-ingest, no data loss. We just need to make it start automatically.

Let me create a systemd unit for it, same pattern as the other two.

arguments.content:
[Unit]
Description=Suricata eve.json -> DuckDB ingester
# Suricata should be writing eve.json before we try to tail it. If suricata
# is installed as a system service, uncomment the next line so we start after it.
# After=suricata.service
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=suricata
Group=suricata
WorkingDirectory=/home/suricata
Environment=PATH=/home/suricata/.local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
# --batch 50000   flush every 50k events
# --read-gap 1.0  hold the write lock open for 1s after closing it (lets the
#                 dashboard acquire the read lock between flushes)
# --reopen-every 1 release the write lock after every batch flush
ExecStart=/usr/bin/python3 /home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.py --eve /var/log/suricata/eve.json --db /home/suricata/eve.duckdb --batch 50000 --read-gap 1.0 --reopen-every 1
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
# Tailing a long-lived file is stateful; if it crashes, systemd restarts it
# and it resumes from the saved offset (idempotent INSERT OR IGNORE).

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

content.text: Successfully wrote 1074 bytes to /home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service

I created the unit file at `/home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service`. It runs the ingester with the same flags you've been using, as the `suricata` user, and restarts on failure.

## What happens on reboot (the answer to your question)

**Before installing this service (current state):**
- `cloudflared.service` ✅ starts automatically
- `streamlit.service` ✅ starts automatically
- `eve_tail2duckdb` ❌ does **not** start — it's just a foreground process
- Result: dashboard loads but shows **stale data** (no new events indexed after the reboot)

**After installing this service:**
- All three start automatically on boot
- The ingester resumes from `eve.duckdb.offset` (4.51 GB) — it does **not** re-process the 4.5 GB of already-ingested events. It just continues tailing from where it left off.
- Even if Suricata's `eve.json` rotated while the VM was down, the ingester handles that (its `Tailer` detects file truncation/rotation and reopens from the start).

## Why re-ingest isn't a problem even if it did restart from zero

Two safety mechanisms are already built in:
1. **Offset persistence** — `eve.duckdb.offset` records the exact byte position in `eve.json` last processed. On restart, the ingester seeks to that offset. No re-read.
2. **Idempotent inserts** — `event_hash` (SHA1 of the raw line) is the primary key, and inserts use `INSERT OR IGNORE`. So even if the offset file were missing and it re-read from the beginning, duplicate lines would be silently ignored. You'd never get double-counted events.

The only real cost of a non-resumed restart would be **wasted time** re-processing 4.5 GB of already-seen events. The offset file avoids that entirely.

## Install the service

First, stop the currently-running foreground ingester so the service can take over without port/lock conflicts:

```bash
kill 58204
```

(If `kill` doesn't work, `kill -9 58204`. Verify it's gone: `ps aux | grep eve_tail | grep -v grep` should print nothing.)

Then install the service:

```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service /etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable eve_tail2duckdb.service
sudo systemctl start eve_tail2duckdb.service
sudo systemctl status eve_tail2duckdb.service
```

You should see `active (running)`.

## Verify it's ingesting

```bash
journalctl -u eve_tail2duckdb.service -f
```

You should see the startup line and, as live traffic flows, periodic `indexed 50000 events (offset=...)` lines. The offset in `eve.duckdb.offset` should stay caught up to `eve.json`'s size.

## Optional: order it after Suricata

If Suricata itself runs as a systemd service on this VM, you can make the ingester wait for it so there's no brief window where it tails a not-yet-existing `eve.json`. Edit the unit:

```bash
sudo systemctl edit eve_tail2duckdb.service
```

Add:
```ini
[Unit]
After=suricata.service
```

Then `sudo systemctl daemon-reload && sudo systemctl restart eve_tail2duckdb.service`. This is optional — without it, the ingester just sleeps and retries if `eve.json` doesn't exist yet (it's already coded for that case). It's a polish step.

## After this, a reboot is fully self-healing

Once the ingester service is installed, a reboot brings up all three services automatically:
1. `cloudflared` → tunnel reconnects (~10s)
2. `streamlit` → dashboard available
3. `eve_tail2duckdb` → resumes indexing from the saved offset

You could `sudo reboot` to test the whole stack comes back, then visit https://suri.yokedostrich.com after ~2 minutes — everything should be live and the dashboard should show fresh data as traffic flows.

The user is asking about the `After=suricata.service` line in the `[Unit]` section that I commented out. Let me look at what I wrote.

In the unit file I created, there's:

The `After=suricata.service` is commented out. The user is asking if it needs to be uncommented.

The answer: it depends on whether Suricata runs as a systemd service on this VM. If it does, uncommenting it makes the ingester wait for Suricata to start first (so eve.json exists). If Suricata doesn't run as a systemd service (e.g., it's run some other way, or it's on a different host, or the VM just reads a file that's always there), then uncommenting it would cause the ingester to wait for a service that doesn't exist — which could delay or block startup.

Actually, if `suricata.service` doesn't exist as a systemd unit, `After=suricata.service` is harmless — systemd just ignores it (it doesn't fail, it just has no ordering dependency if the unit doesn't exist). But it's cleaner to only reference units that exist.

content.text:
[Unit]
Description=Suricata eve.json -> DuckDB ingester
# Suricata should be writing eve.json before we try to tail it. suricata
# is installed as a systemd service (suricata.service), so order after it.
After=suricata.service network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=suricata
Group=suricata
WorkingDirectory=/home/suricata
Environment=PATH=/home/suricata/.local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
# --batch 50000   flush every 50k events
# --read-gap 1.0  hold the write lock open for 1s after closing it (lets the
#                 dashboard acquire the read lock between flushes)
# --reopen-every 1 release the write lock after every batch flush
ExecStart=/usr/bin/python3 /home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.py --eve /var/log/suricata/eve.json --db /home/suricata/eve.duckdb --batch 50000 --read-gap 1.0 --reopen-every 1
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
# Tailing a long-lived file is stateful; if it crashes, systemd restarts it
# and it resumes from the saved offset (idempotent INSERT OR IGNORE).

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Done. The unit now reads:
```
After=suricata.service network-online.target
```

This tells systemd: **start the ingester only after both `suricata.service` and `network-online.target` are up.** On boot, the order becomes:

1. `suricata.service` starts → begins writing `eve.json`
2. `eve_tail2duckdb.service` starts → tails `eve.json` from the saved offset
3. `cloudflared.service` and `streamlit.service` start in parallel (no ordering constraint between them and the ingester)

## Install the service

Stop the current foreground ingester, then install:

```bash
kill 58204
```
(verify it's gone: `ps aux | grep eve_tail | grep -v grep` should print nothing)

```bash
sudo cp /home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service /etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable eve_tail2duckdb.service
sudo systemctl start eve_tail2duckdb.service
sudo systemctl status eve_tail2duckdb.service
```

You should see `active (running)`.

## Verify it's ingesting

```bash
journalctl -u eve_tail2duckdb.service -f
```

You should see the startup line, then `indexed 50000 events (offset=...)` as live traffic flows.

## Why this ordering matters (and why it's safe even if Suricata is slow)

- **Correct ordering:** Suricata starts first, so by the time the ingester runs, `/var/log/suricata/eve.json` exists. No "file not found" retries.
- **Safe even if Suricata takes a moment:** the ingester's code already handles a missing `eve.json` by sleeping and retrying, so even without the ordering it wouldn't crash — but the ordering removes that brief retry window entirely.
- **No ordering needed between ingester and Streamlit:** Streamlit just reads the DuckDB file; it doesn't care whether the ingester is running. Both come up independently.
- **No ordering needed with `cloudflared`:** the tunnel is independent of the data pipeline.

Install it with the commands above and confirm `systemctl status` shows it running. Then all three services are managed by systemd, start on boot in the right order, and restart on crash — a reboot is fully self-healing.

The file at `/home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service` **is** uncommented now — line 5 reads `After=suricata.service network-online.target` with no `#`. If you're still seeing commented lines, you're most likely looking at one of these:

1. **The already-installed copy at `/etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service`** — if you already ran `sudo cp` before I made the edit, that copy has the old (commented) version. The fix only lives in `/home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service` until you re-copy it. Re-run:
   ```bash
   sudo cp /home/suricata/eve_tail2duckdb.service /etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service
   sudo systemctl daemon-reload
   sudo systemctl restart eve_tail2duckdb.service
   ```
   Then verify the installed copy: `grep -A2 "^\[Unit\]" /etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service`

2. **A stale view in your editor** — if you have the file open in `gedit` or another editor, reload it (Ctrl-R / close and reopen).

Check the installed copy with:
```bash
cat /etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service | head -6
```

If that shows the commented `# After=suricata.service` line, you need to re-copy (the command above). If it shows the uncommented `After=suricata.service network-online.target`, you're already on the fixed version.

---

On point 2 — **your message was cut off at "I want to know if the"**. Could you complete the question? I don't want to guess what you're asking. Common things people ask about this setup:

- "...if the `After=` ordering actually waits for Suricata to be *ready* or just *started*?"
- "...if the ingester will miss events during the reboot window?"
- "...if the dashboard needs the ingester to be running to show data?"

Tell me what you wanted to know and I'll answer it precisely.
I want to know if "[Unit]" needs to be uncommented in the file. it currently is

No — **`[Unit]` should stay exactly as it is, with no `#` and nothing to uncomment.** It's already correct.

`[Unit]` is a **section header**, not a setting. Systemd unit files are organized into sections like `[Unit]`, `[Service]`, `[Install]`, and each header must be a bare word in brackets with no `#`. If you commented it out (`# [Unit]`), systemd wouldn't know where the Unit section begins and the file would be invalid.

Here's the structure of the file for clarity:

```ini
[Unit]                    ← section header (NO # — keep as-is)
Description=...
After=suricata.service network-online.target   ← a setting (this is uncommented and correct)
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]                 ← section header (NO #)
Type=simple
User=suricata
...

[Install]                 ← section header (NO #)
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

So to be explicit about every line you might be looking at:

| Line | Should it have `#`? |
|---|---|
| `[Unit]` | **No** — section header, keep bare |
| `# Suricata should be writing eve.json...` | Yes — this is an explanatory comment, leave the `#` |
| `After=suricata.service network-online.target` | **No** — this is the actual setting, must be uncommented (and it is) |
| `Wants=network-online.target` | **No** — setting, keep uncommented |
| `[Service]` | **No** — section header |
| `# --batch 50000 ...` | Yes — comment explaining the flags, leave the `#` |
| `ExecStart=...` | **No** — setting, must be uncommented |

The only lines that should ever have `#` are the **explanatory comments** (the lines starting with `# Suricata should...`, `# --batch...`, etc.). The section headers (`[Unit]`, `[Service]`, `[Install]`) and all the actual settings (`Description=`, `After=`, `ExecStart=`, etc.) must be uncommented.

Your file as it currently stands is correct. If you already copied it to `/etc/systemd/system/`, verify that copy matches:

```bash
head -6 /etc/systemd/system/eve_tail2duckdb.service
```

